name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
وَٱلَّيْلِ إِذَا يَغْشَىٰ (١)
```

```english
By the night when it envelops, (1)
```

```arabic
وَٱلنَّهَارِ إِذَا تَجَلَّىٰ (٢)
```

```english
by the day when it brightens, (2)
```

```arabic
وَمَا خَلَقَ ٱلذَّكَرَ وَٱلْأُنثَىٰٓ (٣)
```

```english
by Him who created the male and the female: (3)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ سَعْيَكُمْ لَشَتَّىٰ (٤)
```

```english
your endeavours are indeed diverse. (4)
```

```arabic
فَأَمَّا مَنْ أَعْطَىٰ وَٱتَّقَىٰ (٥)
```

```english
As for him who gives and is Godwary (5)
```

```arabic
وَصَدَّقَ بِٱلْحُسْنَىٰ (٦)
```

```english
and confirms the best promise, (6)
```

```arabic
فَسَنُيَسِّرُهُۥ لِلْيُسْرَىٰ (٧)
```

```english
We will surely ease him toward facility. (7)
```

```arabic
وَأَمَّا مَنۢ بَخِلَ وَٱسْتَغْنَىٰ (٨)
```

```english
But as for him, who is stingy and self-complacent, (8)
```

```arabic
وَكَذَّبَ بِٱلْحُسْنَىٰ (٩)
```

```english
and denies the best promise, (9)
```

```arabic
فَسَنُيَسِّرُهُۥ لِلْعُسْرَىٰ (١٠)
```

```english
We will surely ease him toward hardship. (10)
```

```arabic
وَمَا يُغْنِى عَنْهُ مَالُهُۥٓ إِذَا تَرَدَّىٰٓ (١١)
```

```english
His wealth will not avail him when he perishes. (11)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ عَلَيْنَا لَلْهُدَىٰ (١٢)
```

```english
Indeed guidance rests with Us, (12)
```

```arabic
وَإِنَّ لَنَا لَلْـَٔاخِرَةَ وَٱلْأُولَىٰ (١٣)
```

```english
and to Us belong the world and the Hereafter. (13)
```

```arabic
فَأَنذَرْتُكُمْ نَارًا تَلَظَّىٰ (١٤)
```

```english
So I warn you of a blazing fire, (14)
```

```arabic
لَا يَصْلَىٰهَآ إِلَّا ٱلْأَشْقَى (١٥)
```

```english
which none shall enter except the most wretched [of persons] (15)
```

```arabic
ٱلَّذِى كَذَّبَ وَتَوَلَّىٰ (١٦)
```

```english
—he who impugns [God’s prophets] and turns his back. (16)
```

```arabic
وَسَيُجَنَّبُهَا ٱلْأَتْقَى (١٧)
```

```english
The Godwary [person] will be spared of that (17)
```

```arabic
ٱلَّذِى يُؤْتِى مَالَهُۥ يَتَزَكَّىٰ (١٨)
```

```english
—he who gives his wealth to purify himself (18)
```

```arabic
وَمَا لِأَحَدٍ عِندَهُۥ مِن نِّعْمَةٍ تُجْزَىٰٓ (١٩)
```

```english
and does not expect any reward from anyone, (19)
```

```arabic
إِلَّا ٱبْتِغَآءَ وَجْهِ رَبِّهِ ٱلْأَعْلَىٰ (٢٠)
```

```english
but seek only the pleasure of their Lord, the Most Exalted, (20)
```

```arabic
وَلَسَوْفَ يَرْضَىٰ (٢١)
```

```english
and, surely, soon they will be well-pleased. (21)
```

## (Commentary)

The purpose of the Surah is warning (الإنذار), and it pursues this by pointing to the diversity of human endeavors: that among them are those who spend, maintain godwariness (اتقى), and believe in the ultimate good (الحسنى), whom Allah will facilitate toward an eternal, happy life; and among them are those who are stingy, consider themselves self-sufficient (استغنى), and deny the ultimate good, whom Allah will lead toward a wretched end. The Surah shows particular concern and special attention to the matter of financial spending (الإنفاق).

The Surah could be either Meccan or Medinan based on its context.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَاللَّيْلِ إِذا يَغْشى** — *By the night when it enshrouds (92:1)*: This is an oath by the night when it enshrouds the day, similar to His saying, the Exalted: **يُغْشِي اللَّيْلَ النَّهارَ** — *He makes the night cover the day (7:54)*. It is also possible that what is meant is its enshrouding of the earth or the sun.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَالنَّهارِ إِذا تَجَلَّى** — *by the day when it brightens (92:2)*: This is conjoined to "the night." Manifestation (التجلي) is the appearance of a thing after its concealment. The use of the present tense for the attribute of the night (**يَغْشى**) and the past tense for the attribute of the day (**تَجَلَّى**) was previously explained in the commentary at the beginning of the preceding Surah.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَما خَلَقَ الذَّكَرَ وَالْأُنْثى** — *by Him who created the male and the female (92:3)*: This is conjoined to "the night" like the previous verse. The word **ما** is a relative pronoun referring to Allah, the Glorified. It is expressed as **ما** (that which) rather than **من** (he who) to favor ambiguity, which suggests veneration (التعظيم) and magnification (التفخيم). The meaning is: "I swear by the Wondrous Being who brought into existence the male and the female, who are different despite being of the same species."

It has been said that **ما** is infinitive (مصدرية), meaning: "I swear by the creation of the male and the female," but this is a weak interpretation.

What is meant by "the male and the female" is the absolute male and female wherever they exist. It has been said it refers to the male and female of humans, and it has been said it refers to Adam and his spouse Eve (حواء). The most plausible of these views is the first.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّ سَعْيَكُمْ لَشَتَّى** — *your endeavors are indeed diverse (92:4)*: Endeavor (السعي) is rapid walking, and here it refers to action (العمل) insofar as it is an object of concern; it carries a collective meaning. Diverse (**شتى**) is the plural of **شتيت**, meaning scattered or varied, just as **مرضى** (sick) is the plural of **مريض**.

This sentence is the response to the oath (جواب القسم). The meaning is: "I swear by these things that are diverse in their creation and effects, that your endeavors are indeed diverse in themselves and in their consequences. Among them is giving, godwariness, and belief, which have their specific effect; and among them is stinginess, self-sufficiency, and denial, which have their specific effect."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَأَمَّا مَنْ أَعْطى وَاتَّقى وَصَدَّقَ بِالْحُسْنى فَسَنُيَسِّرُهُ لِلْيُسْرى** — *As for him who gives and is God-wary, and admits the ultimate good, We shall surely ease him into facility (92:5-7)*: This is a detailed explanation of the diversity of their endeavors and the difference in their effects.

What is meant by "giving" (الإعطاء) is spending wealth for the sake of Allah, evidenced by its contrast with "stinginess" (البخل), which is apparent in withholding the spending of wealth, and by His subsequent saying: **وَما يُغْنِي عَنْهُ مالُهُ إِذا تَرَدَّى** — *His wealth will not avail him when he perishes (92:11)*.

His saying: **وَاتَّقى** — *and is God-wary* acts as an explanation for "giving," indicating that what is meant is giving by way of religious godwariness (التقوى الدينية).

His saying: **وَصَدَّقَ بِالْحُسْنى** — *and admits the ultimate good*: The word "ultimate good" (الحسنى) is an adjective standing in place of the noun it describes. It appears that the implied meaning is "the most beautiful promise" (العدة الحسنى), which is the reward Allah has promised for spending for His Noble Face. This constitutes belief in the Resurrection (البعث) and faith in it, which necessitates faith in His Oneness (التوحيد) in Lordship and Divinity, as well as faith in the Message (الرسالة), for it is the path through which Allah's promise of reward is reached.

The sum of the two verses is that one should be a believer in Allah, His Messenger, and the Last Day, and spend wealth for the sake of Allah, seeking His reward which He promised through the tongue of His Messenger.

His saying: **فَسَنُيَسِّرُهُ لِلْيُسْرى** — *We shall surely ease him into facility*: Facilitation (التيسير) is preparation and readiness. Facility (اليسرى) is the trait in which there is ease without hardship; describing it as "easy" involves a type of metaphor. Thus, facilitating him for facility means granting him success (التوفيق) for righteous deeds (الأعمال الصالحة) by making them easy for him without difficulty, or making him prepared for a happy life with his Lord and entry into Paradise because of the righteous deeds he performs. The second view is closer and more clearly aligned with the established promises of the Quran.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَأَمَّا مَنْ بَخِلَ وَاسْتَغْنى وَكَذَّبَ بِالْحُسْنى فَسَنُيَسِّرُهُ لِلْعُسْرى وَما يُغْنِي عَنْهُ مالُهُ إِذا تَرَدَّى** — *But as for him who is stingy and considers himself self-sufficient, and denies the ultimate good, We shall surely ease him into hardship. His wealth will not avail him when he perishes (92:8-11)*: Stinginess (البخل) is the opposite of giving. Self-sufficiency (الاستغناء) is seeking wealth and riches through withholding and accumulating. Denial of the ultimate good means disbelief in the most beautiful promise and the reward of Allah conveyed by the Prophets and Messengers, which reverts to the denial of the Resurrection.

Facilitating him for hardship (العسرى) means forsaking him (الخذلان) by not granting him success for righteous deeds—by making them heavy upon him and not expanding his breast for faith—or preparing him for punishment.

His saying: **وَما يُغْنِي عَنْهُ مالُهُ إِذا تَرَدَّى** — *His wealth will not avail him when he perishes*: Perishing (التردي) is falling from a high place, and it is also used for destruction. What is meant is his falling into the pit of the grave, or into Hell, or his destruction.

The word **ما** is either interrogative or negative: "What thing will his wealth avail him when he dies and perishes?" or "His wealth will not avail him when he dies and perishes."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّ عَلَيْنا لَلْهُدى وَإِنَّ لَنا لَلْآخِرَةَ وَالْأُولى** — *Indeed it is for Us to guide, and to Us belong the Hereafter and the world (92:12-13)*: This is a justification for what preceded regarding the facilitation toward facility and hardship, or an announcement of it in the briefest manner. Its essence is: "We only perform this facilitation or provide this clarification because it is part of guidance (الهدى), and guidance is incumbent upon Us; nothing competes with Us in that, nor does any preventer hinder Us."

His saying: **إِنَّ عَلَيْنا لَلْهُدى** — *Indeed it is for Us to guide* indicates that the guidance of people is among the things the Glorified has decreed and made obligatory upon Himself by the requirement of Wisdom (الحكمة). This is because He created them to worship Him, as He said: **وَما خَلَقْتُ الْجِنَّ وَالْإِنْسَ إِلَّا لِيَعْبُدُونِ** — *I did not create the jinn and the humans except that they may worship Me (51:56)*. Thus, He made His worship the goal of their creation and made it a straight path (صراطاً مستقيماً) to Him, as He said: **إِنَّ اللهَ رَبِّي وَرَبُّكُمْ فَاعْبُدُوهُ هذا صِراطٌ مُسْتَقِيمٌ** — *Indeed Allah is my Lord and your Lord; so worship Him. This is a straight path (3:51)*, and He said: **وَإِنَّكَ لَتَهْدِي إِلى صِراطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ صِراطِ اللهِ** — *Indeed you guide to a straight path, the path of Allah (42:52-53)*. He decreed upon Himself to clarify His way to them and guide them to it in the sense of "showing the way" (إراءة الطريق), whether they follow it or abandon it, as He said: **وَعَلَى اللهِ قَصْدُ السَّبِيلِ وَمِنْها جائِرٌ** — *With Allah rests the [direction of the] right way, and some of them are devious (16:9)*, and He said: **وَاللهُ يَقُولُ الْحَقَّ وَهُوَ يَهْدِي السَّبِيلَ** — *Allah speaks the truth and He guides to the way (33:4)*, and He said: **إِنَّا هَدَيْناهُ السَّبِيلَ إِمَّا شاكِراً وَإِمَّا كَفُوراً** — *Indeed We have guided him to the way, whether he be grateful or ungrateful (76:3)*. This does not contradict others besides Allah performing this type of guidance by His permission, such as the Prophets, as He said: **وَإِنَّكَ لَتَهْدِي إِلى صِراطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ** — *Indeed you guide to a straight path (42:52)*, and He said: **قُلْ هذِهِ سَبِيلِي أَدْعُوا إِلَى اللهِ عَلى بَصِيرَةٍ أَنَا وَمَنِ اتَّبَعَنِي** — *Say, ‘This is my way: I summon to Allah with insight—I and he who follows me’ (12:108)*.

A rational explanation of this matter was previously provided in the discussions on Prophethood (النبوة) in the second volume of this book.

This concerns guidance in the sense of "showing the way." As for guidance in the sense of "conveying to the goal" (الإيصال إلى المطلوب)—the goal here being the beautiful effects that result from being guided by Allah's guidance and being characterized by servitude (العبودية), such as the immediate good life in this world and the eternal happy life in the Hereafter—it is clearly of the nature of creation and origination (الإيجاد) which is exclusive to Him. Thus, it is among what Allah has decreed, made obligatory upon Himself, and recorded with His true promise. He said: **فَمَنِ اتَّبَعَ هُدايَ فَلا يَضِلُّ وَلا يَشْقى** — *Then whoever follows My guidance will not go astray, nor will he be wretched (20:123)*, and He said: **مَنْ عَمِلَ صالِحاً مِنْ ذَكَرٍ أَوْ أُنْثى وَهُوَ مُؤْمِنٌ فَلَنُحْيِيَنَّهُ حَياةً طَيِّبَةً وَلَنَجْزِيَنَّهُمْ أَجْرَهُمْ بِأَحْسَنِ ما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ** — *Whoever acts righteously, [whether] male or female, should he be faithful, We shall surely revive him with a good life and pay them their reward by the best of what they used to do (16:97)*, and He said: **وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ سَنُدْخِلُهُمْ جَنَّاتٍ تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِهَا الْأَنْهارُ خالِدِينَ فِيها أَبَداً وَعْدَ اللهِ حَقًّا وَمَنْ أَصْدَقُ مِنَ اللهِ قِيلاً** — *But those who have faith and do righteous deeds, We shall admit them into gardens with conduits running with them, to remain in them forever—a true promise of Allah, and who is more truthful in speech than Allah? (4:122)*.

The attribution of this meaning of guidance to Allah by way of originality (الأصالة) does not contradict its attribution to others by way of dependency (التبع) through the mediation of causes between Him and the effect attributed to them by His permission.

The meaning of the verse—if guidance (الهدى) means "showing the way"—is: "We only clarify to you what We clarify because it is part of showing the way of servitude, and showing the way is incumbent upon Us." If it means "conveying to the goal," it is: "We only facilitate these people for the facility of righteous deeds or the eternal easy life and entry into Paradise because it is part of conveying things to their ends, and that is incumbent upon Us."

As for facilitation toward hardship (العسرى), it is something upon which facilitation toward facility depends: **لِيَمِيزَ اللهُ الْخَبِيثَ مِنَ الطَّيِّبِ وَيَجْعَلَ الْخَبِيثَ بَعْضَهُ عَلى بَعْضٍ فَيَرْكُمَهُ جَمِيعاً فَيَجْعَلَهُ فِي جَهَنَّمَ** — *so that Allah may separate the bad from the good, and pile up the bad on one another, and heap them all together, and cast them into hell (8:37)*. He has said in the Quran, which is a guidance for the worlds: **وَنُنَزِّلُ مِنَ الْقُرْآنِ ما هُوَ شِفاءٌ وَرَحْمَةٌ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَلا يَزِيدُ الظَّالِمِينَ إِلَّا خَساراً** — *We send down in the Quran that which is a cure and mercy for the faithful, and it increases the wrongdoers only in loss (17:82)*.

It is possible that what is meant is absolute guidance, encompassing both real ontological guidance (الهداية التكوينية الحقيقية) and conventional legislative guidance (التشريعية الاعتبارية)—as suggested by the absolute nature of the term. To Allah belongs real guidance, as He said: **الَّذِي أَعْطى كُلَّ شَيْءٍ خَلْقَهُ ثُمَّ هَدى** — *He who gave everything its creation and then guided it (20:50)*, and conventional guidance, as He said: **إِنَّا هَدَيْناهُ السَّبِيلَ إِمَّا شاكِراً وَإِمَّا كَفُوراً** — *Indeed We have guided him to the way, whether he be grateful or ungrateful (76:3)*.

His saying: **وَإِنَّ لَنا لَلْآخِرَةَ وَالْأُولى** — *and to Us belong the Hereafter and the world*: That is, the realm of the beginning and the realm of the return. Everything to which the term "thing" applies is owned by Him through real ownership (الملك الحقيقي), which is the subsistence of its existence by its Sustaining Lord (الرب القيوم). From this branches conventional ownership (الملك الاعتباري), whose effects include the permissibility of disposal.

Thus, He owns everything from every aspect; nothing else owns anything of it. No opponent can oppose Him, no preventer can hinder Him, and nothing can overcome Him, as He said: **وَاللهُ يَحْكُمُ لا مُعَقِّبَ لِحُكْمِهِ** — *Allah judges, and there is no one to revise His judgment (13:41)*, and He said: **وَاللهُ غالِبٌ عَلى أَمْرِهِ** — *Allah is dominant over His affairs (12:21)*, and He said: **وَيَفْعَلُ اللهُ ما يَشاءُ** — *and Allah does whatever He wishes (14:27)*.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَأَنْذَرْتُكُمْ ناراً تَلَظَّى لا يَصْلاها إِلَّا الْأَشْقَى الَّذِي كَذَّبَ وَتَوَلَّى** — *So I warn you of a blazing fire, which none shall enter except the most wretched, who denies and turns away (92:14-16)*: This is a derivation from what preceded. That is, since guidance is incumbent upon Us, I have warned you of the fire of Hell. This explains the shift (الالتفات) in His saying **فَأَنْذَرْتُكُمْ** (So I warn you) from speaking in the plural ("We") to speaking in the singular ("I"): since guidance is a decreed and inevitable matter, the Warner by way of originality is Allah, even if it is through the tongue of His Messenger.

The blazing of the fire (**تلظى**) is its flaming and intense heat. What is meant by the blazing fire is Hell, as He said: **كَلَّا إِنَّها لَظى** — *No indeed! It is a blazing flame (70:15)*.

What is meant by "the most wretched" (الأشقى) is the absolute disbeliever who disbelieves by denial and turning away. He is more wretched than all others who are wretched in their worldly life. One afflicted in his body is wretched; one struck in his wealth or children, for example, is wretched; and one who loses in the matter of his Hereafter is wretched. The one wretched in the matter of his Hereafter is the most wretched of all because his wretchedness is eternal with no hope of escape, unlike wretchedness in any worldly affair, which is inevitably cut short and expected to vanish soon.

Thus, "the most wretched" refers to the disbeliever who denies the true call and turns away from it, as indicated by his description: **الَّذِي كَذَّبَ وَتَوَلَّى** — *who denies and turns away*. This is supported by the absolute nature of the warning. As for "the most wretched" meaning the most wretched of all people, the context does not support this at all.

What is meant by "entering" (صلي) the fire is following it and remaining in it, which conveys the meaning of eternity (الخلود), something Allah has decreed for the disbeliever. He said: **وَالَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَكَذَّبُوا بِآياتِنا أُولئِكَ أَصْحابُ النَّارِ هُمْ فِيها خالِدُونَ** — *As for those who are faithless and deny Our signs, they shall be the inmates of the Fire and they shall remain in it [forever] (2:39)*.

By this, the objection is refuted that His saying: **لا يَصْلاها إِلَّا الْأَشْقَى** — *none shall enter it except the most wretched* excludes the punishment of the fire for sinful believers (فساق المؤمنين) based on the restriction (القصر) in the verse. The refutation is that the verse only denies eternity in it for those other than the disbeliever, not the initial entry itself.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَسَيُجَنَّبُهَا الْأَتْقَى الَّذِي يُؤْتِي مالَهُ يَتَزَكَّى وَما لِأَحَدٍ عِنْدَهُ مِنْ نِعْمَةٍ تُجْزى** — *But the most God-wary will be spared it, he who gives his wealth to purify himself, and does not expect any reward from anyone (92:17-19)*: Sparing (التجنيب) is distancing. The pronoun in **سَيُجَنَّبُهَا** refers to the fire. The meaning is: the most God-conscious will be distanced from the fire.

What is meant by "the most God-conscious" (الأتقى) is the one who is more God-conscious than others among those who avoid dangers. There are those who avoid the loss of lives like death and killing, those who avoid the ruin of wealth, and those who avoid non-existence and poverty and thus withhold from giving wealth, and so on. Among them are those who are God-conscious and thus give wealth for His sake. Or if you wish, say: he avoids the loss of the Hereafter and thus purifies himself by giving.

Thus, the one over whom "the most God-conscious" is preferred is the one who does not maintain godwariness by giving wealth, even if he avoids other worldly dangers or is God-conscious in other righteous deeds.

The verse is general in its significance, not specific. This is indicated by the description of "the most God-conscious" as: **الَّذِي يُؤْتِي مالَهُ** — *he who gives his wealth*, etc., which is a general description, as is what follows it. This does not contradict the verses or the entire Surah being revealed for a specific reason, as mentioned in the occasions of revelation (أسباب النزول).

As for making the comparison so absolute that it includes all people, whether wicked or righteous—which would necessitate that the one preferred be restricted to a single individual absolutely or one in every age—such that the meaning would be "the one who is the most God-conscious of all people will be spared it," and similarly for "none shall enter it except the most wretched of all people," the context of the verses at the beginning of the Surah does not support this. Nor does the general warning in His saying: **فَأَنْذَرْتُكُمْ ناراً تَلَظَّى** — *So I warn you of a blazing fire*. It would make no sense to say: "I warn you all of a fire in which none shall remain forever except one of you all, and from which none shall be saved except one of you all."

His saying: **الَّذِي يُؤْتِي مالَهُ يَتَزَكَّى** — *he who gives his wealth to purify himself* is an attribute of "the most God-conscious," meaning the one who gives and spends his wealth seeking thereby to grow with a righteous growth.

His saying: **وَما لِأَحَدٍ عِنْدَهُ مِنْ نِعْمَةٍ تُجْزى** — *and does not expect any reward from anyone* is a confirmation of the content of the previous verse. That is, no one has a favor (نعمة) with him that is being repaid (تجزى) by the wealth he gives; rather, he gives it solely for the sake of Allah. This meaning is supported by the subsequent phrase: **إِلَّا ابْتِغاءَ وَجْهِ رَبِّهِ الْأَعْلى** — *but only seeking the pleasure of his Lord, the Most Exalted (92:20)*.

The implied meaning is: "of a favor to be repaid by it." The prepositional phrase was omitted to maintain the rhyme (الفواصل). This refutes the claim that **تُجْزى** is in the passive voice because the intent is not a specific actor.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِلَّا ابْتِغاءَ وَجْهِ رَبِّهِ الْأَعْلى** — *but only seeking the pleasure of his Lord, the Most Exalted (92:20)*: This is a disconnected exception (استثناء منقطع). The meaning is: "But he gives his wealth seeking the Face (وجه) of his Lord, the Most Exalted." Discussion has already passed regarding the meaning of the Face of Allah and the meaning of the Most Exalted Name.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلَسَوْفَ يَرْضى** — *and he will soon be well-pleased (92:21)*: That is, this most God-conscious person will soon be well-pleased with the abundant reward and the beautiful, good recompense that his Most Exalted Lord bestows upon him.

In mentioning the attributes "Lord" (الرب) and "Most Exalted" (الأعلى), there is an indication that the recompense he is given is the most pleasant and highest recompense, which is befitting of His Lordship and His Exaltation. From here, the reason for the shift (الالتفات) in the previous verse in His saying: **وَجْهِ رَبِّهِ الْأَعْلى** — *the Face of his Lord, the Most Exalted* becomes clear, moving from the context of speaking in the first person to the third person by referring to the two attributes: "his Lord, the Most Exalted."

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of transmission from Muhammad ibn Muslim, who said: "I said to Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him): 'The saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **وَاللَّيْلِ إِذا يَغْشى** (By the night when it enshrouds) and **وَالنَّجْمِ إِذا هَوى** (By the star when it sets) and what is similar to that?' He said: 'Indeed, Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, may swear by whatever He wishes of His creation, but His creation may not swear except by Him.'"

**I say:** It was also narrated in *al-Faqih* with its chain of transmission from Ali ibn Mahziyar from Abu Ja'far the Second (peace be upon him).

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَاللَّيْلِ إِذا يَغْشى** — *By the night when it enshrouds*, he said: "When it enshrouds the day, and it is an oath."

From al-Himyari in *Qurb al-Isnad*, from Ahmad ibn Muhammad, from Ahmad ibn Muhammad ibn Abi Nasr, from Abu al-Hasan al-Rida (peace be upon him), who said: "I heard him saying regarding the interpretation of **وَاللَّيْلِ إِذا يَغْشى** (By the night when it enshrouds): 'A man had a palm tree in another man's garden and he used to cause him harm. The man complained about this to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), who summoned the owner and said: "Give me your palm tree in exchange for a palm tree in Paradise," but he refused. A man from the Ansar, nicknamed Abu al-Dahdah, heard this and went to the owner of the palm tree and said: "Sell me your palm tree for my garden," and he sold it to him. He then came to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and said: "O Messenger of Allah, I have bought so-and-so's palm tree for my garden." The Messenger of Allah said: "You shall have a palm tree in Paradise in its stead."

Then Allah, the Exalted, revealed to His Prophet: **وَما خَلَقَ الذَّكَرَ وَالْأُنْثى ـ إِنَّ سَعْيَكُمْ لَشَتَّى فَأَمَّا مَنْ أَعْطى** (by Him who created the male and the female—your endeavors are indeed diverse. As for him who gives)—meaning the palm tree—**وَاتَّقى وَصَدَّقَ بِالْحُسْنى** (and is God-wary, and admits the ultimate good)—which is what is with the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم)—**فَسَنُيَسِّرُهُ لِلْيُسْرى** (We shall surely ease him into facility) until His saying: **تَرَدَّى** (he perishes).'"

**I say:** Al-Qummi narrated it in his *Tafsir* as a disconnected (مرسلاً) and anonymous (مضمراً) report. His saying "the two spouses" is his (peace be upon him) interpretation of "the male and the female."

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَسَيُجَنَّبُهَا الْأَتْقَى** — *But the most God-wary will be spared it*, he said: "Abu al-Dahdah."

**I say:** This is what has been narrated through Shi'i paths from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them).

Al-Tabarsi narrated the story in *Majma' al-Bayan* from al-Wahidi with his chain of transmission from Ikrimah from Ibn Abbas, and in it, the Ansari bargained with the owner of the palm tree for his tree, then bought it from him for forty palm trees, then gifted it to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), and the Prophet gifted it to the owner of the house. Then al-Tabarsi narrated from Ata that the man's name was Abu al-Dahdah. Al-Suyuti narrated the story in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from Ibn Abi Hatim from Ibn Abbas and graded it weak.

It has been narrated through Sunni paths that the Surah was revealed concerning Abu Bakr. Al-Razi said in *al-Tafsir al-Kabir*: "The commentators among us are in consensus that what is meant by it—meaning by 'the most God-wary'—is Abu Bakr. Know that the Shi'a in their entirety deny this narration and say it was revealed concerning Ali ibn Abi Talib, and the evidence for it is His saying, the Exalted: **وَيُؤْتُونَ الزَّكاةَ وَهُمْ راكِعُونَ** (and give the zakat while they bow). Thus, His saying: **الْأَتْقَى الَّذِي يُؤْتِي مالَهُ يَتَزَكَّى** (the most God-wary, he who gives his wealth to purify himself) is an allusion to what is in that verse: **وَيُؤْتُونَ الزَّكاةَ وَهُمْ راكِعُونَ** (and give the zakat while they bow)." Then he took "the most God-wary" to mean the best of creation, i.e., the most God-conscious of all people, and discussion on this has already passed.

As for what is attributed to the Shi'a in their entirety, what is relied upon from their paths is the authentic report of al-Himyari mentioned above and similar narrations indicating its revelation concerning Abu al-Dahdah al-Ansari.

Yes, it has appeared in a weak narration from al-Barqi from Isma'il ibn Mihran from Ayman ibn Muhriz from Abu Basir from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), in which it says: "As for His saying: **وَسَيُجَنَّبُهَا الْأَتْقَى** (But the most God-wary will be spared it), he said: 'The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and those who followed him.' And **الَّذِي يُؤْتِي مالَهُ يَتَزَكَّى** (he who gives his wealth to purify himself), he said: 'That is the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him), and it is His saying: **وَيُؤْتُونَ الزَّكاةَ وَهُمْ راكِعُونَ** (and give the zakat while they bow).' And His saying: **وَما لِأَحَدٍ عِنْدَهُ مِنْ نِعْمَةٍ تُجْزى** (and does not expect any reward from anyone), he is the Messenger of Allah, for whom no one has a favor to be repaid—and his favor is continuous upon all creation (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم)."

The narration, despite the weakness of its chain, is of the nature of "application" (الجري والتطبيق) rather than "interpretation" (التفسير). A clear evidence for this is its application of the described to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), the attribute to Ali (peace be upon him), and then the following verse to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم); if it were interpretation, the order would certainly be corrupted. This is assuming the "and" in his saying "and **الَّذِي يُؤْتِي مالَهُ يَتَزَكَّى**" is from the narration; if it were assumed to be from the verse, the narration would be among the rejected narrations of alteration (التحريف).

From al-Himyari from Ahmad ibn Muhammad from Ahmad ibn Muhammad ibn Abi Nasr from Abu al-Hasan al-Rida (peace be upon him), who said: "I said: 'The saying of Allah, Blessed and Exalted: **إِنَّ عَلَيْنا لَلْهُدى** (Indeed it is for Us to guide)?' He said: 'Indeed, Allah guides whom He wills and leads astray whom He wills.'"

I said to him: "May Allah set you right, a group of our companions claim that knowledge (المعرفة) is acquired (مكتسبة), and that if they look from the perspective of reasoning (النظر), they will attain it."

He denied that and said: "What is the matter with these people that they do not acquire good for themselves? There is no one among people except that he wishes to be better than one who is better than him. These are the Banu Hashim; their position is their position and their kinship is their kinship, and they are more entitled to this matter than you. Do you think they do not look out for themselves? Yet you have known while they have not."

Abu Ja'far said: "If people were able, they would have loved us."

**I say:** As for guidance (الهداية)—meaning conveying to the goal—it belongs to Allah, the Exalted, because it is among the affairs of Lordship. As for leading astray (الإضلال)—meaning leading astray by way of recompense (المجازاة) rather than initial leading astray, which is not attributed to Him, the Exalted—it is also from Allah, as it is a withholding of the descent of mercy and an absence of guidance. Since guidance belongs to Him, the withholding of it is also attributed to Him, the Exalted.
